POSIX Message  
Queues  
Inter-Process Communication with  
Prioritized Messages

What is a POSIX Message Queue?  
• IPC mechanism that allows processes to exchange messages  
• Messages have fixed maximum size and user-defined priority  
• Kernel stores messages inside a managed queue  
• Accessible through a name (like /myqueue)  
• Faster and simpler than sockets for small, structured messages  
Process A  
Process B  
Message Queue  
Why Use Message Queues?  
Need to send structured  
data, not raw bytes  
Want asynchronous  
communication  
Want priorities (urgent  
messages delivered first)  
Useful in multi-process  
embedded applications  
Avoid complexity of pipes or  
(sensor tasks,  
command/control, alarms,  
events)  
shared memory  
Key Characteristics  
• Queue lives inside kernel  
• Identified by a name (/mq_name)  
• Supports blocking & non-blocking operations  
• Supports synchronous notifications (signals or threads)  
• Message order = by priority, then FIFO  
• Must be created and configured using attributes  
Message Queue APIs (mq_xxx)  
• mq_open() → create/open  
• mq_send() → send message  
• mq_receive() → receive message  
• mq_getattr() / mq_setattr() → view/modify attributes  
• mq_notify() → signal/thread notification  
• mq_close() → close MQ descriptor  
• mq_unlink() → delete queue  
Creating a Queue  
struct mq_attr attr;  
attr.mq_flags = 0;  
attr.mq_maxmsg = 10;  
attr.mq_msgsize = 64;  
attr.mq_curmsgs = 0;  
mqd_t q = mq_open("/apexq", O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0666, &attr);  
• Name must start with '/’  
• Queue persists until mq_unlink()  
Sending Messages (mq_send)  
mq_send(q, "Hello", 6, 2); // message, length, priority  
• Priority: 0–31 (higher delivered first)  
• Blocks if queue full  
• Non-blocking mode → returns EAGAIN  
• Message larger than msgsize → error (EMSGSIZE)  
Receiving Messages (mq_receive)  
char buf[64];  
unsigned int prio;  
mq_receive(q, buf, sizeof(buf), &prio);  
• Behavior:  
• Blocks if queue empty  
• Non-blocking → EAGAIN  
• Returns length of received message  
• Outputs priority of message  
Message Priorities  
• Priorities allow urgency-based message retrieval  
• Higher priority messages dequeued first  
• Within same priority → FIFO order  
• Useful for:  
• Alarms  
• emergency stop events  
• high-frequency sensor updates  
Blocking vs Non-blocking  
• Blocking mode: (default)  
• mq_send() waits if queue full  
• mq_receive() waits if queue empty  
• Non-blocking mode:  
• mq_send() → EAGAIN on full  
• mq_receive() → EAGAIN on empty  
• Enable non-blocking:  
• Use O_NONBLOCK in mq_open()  
• Or change via mq_setattr()  
Queue Attributes (Inspection)  
struct mq_attr info;  
mq_getattr(q, &info);  
• mq_curmsgs → current messages  
• mq_maxmsg → capacity  
• mq_msgsize → message size limit  
• Useful for monitoring & debugging.  
Cleaning Up Queues  
• mq_close(q); → close descriptor  
• mq_unlink("/apexq"); → remove queue from system  
Queue persists in kernel until unlink, even if all processes exit.  
Always unlink after labs.  
Example: Sender and Receiver  
mqd_t q = mq_open("/apexq", O_WRONLY);  
mq_send(q, "SPEED=40", 9, 1);  
mq_close(q);  
mqd_t q = mq_open("/apexq", O_RDONLY);  
char buf[64]; unsigned int p;  
int n = mq_receive(q, buf, 64, &p);  
printf("Msg: %s, Priority: %u\n", buf, p);  
mq_close(q);  
separate sender process uses same queue name.  
Error Conditions  
• Queue full → mq_send → EAGAIN or block  
• Queue empty → mq_receive → EAGAIN or block  
• Wrong queue name → ENOENT  
• Exceeding msgsize → EMSGSIZE  
• No permissions → EACCES  
• Queue deleted while open → operations fail  
Best Practices  
• Define a message struct instead of raw text  
• Keep messages small (<100 bytes)  
• Avoid heavy binary blobs (use shared memory instead)  
• Always close + unlink in cleanup  
• Use priorities carefully (not everything should be prio 31)  
Use Cases  
• Sensor → Controller queue  
• Event dispatcher  
• Logging framework  
• Autonomous robot state updates  
• Diagnostics & notification events  
• Multi-process architectures (ECU services)  
Example 1: Basic MQ Send/Receive  
Task:  
• Create queue /labq  
• Write one sender, one receiver  
• Send 5 messages  
• Receiver prints them in arrival order  
Example 2: Using Priorities  
• Sender sends messages with priorities 1, 5, 3  
• Receiver should print: p5, p3, p1  
• Then delete queue  
Learning: priority-based ordering  
Example 3: Multi-Process Fan-In Queue  
Setup:  
• 3 producer processes → send events  
• 1 consumer → prints all  
• Use priorities for severity levels (INFO/WARN/CRITICAL)  
Learning: multi-process MQ usage  
Common Pitfalls  
• Forgetting to unlink queue → leftover kernel objects  
• Using too large msgsize → heavy overhead  
• Ignoring return values  
• Overusing high priorities  
• Trying to use MQ for streaming or huge data  
Summary  
• POSIX MQs provide structured, prioritized message IPC  
• Easy for unrelated processes  
• Blocking & non-blocking modes available  
• Supports notifications  
• Great for event-driven systems and modular architectures  
Scenario: Prioritized Event Bus for Alerts  
(1/4)  
Context  
• Develop an event bus using a named POSIX message queue /apex_events.  
• Multiple producers send events (INFO/WARN/CRITICAL) with priorities; a single consumer receives and  
handles them in priority order.  
Scenario: Prioritized Event Bus for Alerts  
(2/4)  
PART 1 — Queue Setup (Consumer)  
• Create event_consumer.c.  
• mq_open("/apex_events", O_CREAT | O_RDONLY | O_NONBLOCK, 0666, &attr)  
• mq_maxmsg = 16, mq_msgsize = 128  
• Loop: mq_receive messages; if none, sleep 100 ms; print:  
[prio=<p>] <timestamp> <source> <msg>  
Scenario: Prioritized Event Bus for Alerts  
(3/4)  
PART 2 — Producer(s)  
• Create event_producer.c.  
• CLI: ./event_producer <prio> <source> <msg...>  
• Map prio: CRITICAL=5, WARN=3, INFO=1.  
• mq_send(q, payload, len, prio)  
Scenario: Prioritized Event Bus for Alerts  
(4/4)  
PART 3 — Notification  
• In consumer, register mq_notify (signal or thread).  
• On transition from empty→non-empty, wake up and drain the queue immediately.
